case_name: Morrison v Liverpool Women's NHS Foundation Trust [2020] EWHC 91 (QB) (27 January 2020)

summary.brief: Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The claimant suffered a serious injury to her womb and had to undergo a hysterectomy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Following her second admission, the claimant was assessed by the defendant's clinicians on four occasions: 2:40 am, 4:10 am, 5:35 am and 7:05 am.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The claimant's expert, Mr Waterstone, concluded that no reasonable body of medical opinion could support the decision not to proceed to a caesarean section following the appraisal at 4:10 am.
evidence_index.speaker: Mr Waterstone

evidence_index.text: Mr Irons, on behalf of the defendant, concluded that a caesarean section was not mandated at this time.
evidence_index.speaker: Mr Irons

evidence_index.text: The Recorder was entitled to conclude that the failure to act at 4:10 am amounted to a breach of duty.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Recorder observed: 'Mr Waterstone approached the matter from an objective, practical perspective ...'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The claimant was expecting her fourth child. She had a history of undergoing two previous caesarean sections and had also sustained a tear to her womb.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On 2 December 2014, her waters broke and she was admitted to the defendant's hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Recorder correctly observed that both Mr Waterstone and Mr Irons were of the view that it was highly unlikely that contractions would continue following a rupture of the uterus.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.chief_complaint: In labor with history of previous caesarean sections
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient was expecting her fourth child, with a history of two previous caesarean sections and a tear to her womb. Her pregnancy was categorized as high risk. She presented to the hospital in the latent stage of labor.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: High-risk pregnancy with potential for uterine rupture

reveal.result_summary: Decision not to proceed with caesarean section
reveal.decision_impact: Contributed to delay in performing caesarean section

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to hospital in latent labor

timeline_phases.name: Labor Monitoring
timeline_phases.description: Monitoring of labor progression and decision-making

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in latent labor with high-risk pregnancy
decision_points.prompt: Should a caesarean section be performed at 4:10 am?

options.clinical_reasoning: High risk of uterine rupture; timely intervention needed
Assumed labor was not yet established

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed caesarean was not yet necessary

expected_action_court.description: Caesarean section should have been performed
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence indicated risk of uterine rupture

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to act on high-risk indicators
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Caesarean section should have been performed at 4:10 am

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered uterine rupture and required hysterectomy

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to perform caesarean section at 4:10 am
Causation established - delay led to uterine rupture

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to perform caesarean section at 4:10 am
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Timely caesarean would have prevented rupture

factual_timeline.event: Patient admitted to hospital after waters broke
Patient presents to hospital in latent labor

allegations.allegation: Failure to perform caesarean section at 4:10 am
